sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hip and groin pain may come from joint, tendon, muscle, lumbar referral, abdominal causes, or fracture. The priority is distinguishing benign overload from serious joint, fracture, or abdominal pathology.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Groin, buttock, lateral hip, or anterior thigh pain
- Pain with walking, stairs, running, turning, or getting in and out of vehicles
- Reduced hip range or limp
- Possible clicking, instability, or adductor-related pain

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Escalate for inability to weight-bear after trauma, suspected neck-of-femur injury, septic joint, severe night pain, abdominal or testicular referral, or neurovascular change
- Older patients and patients on steroids or with osteoporosis need a low threshold for fracture imaging
- Lumbar referral and intra-abdominal causes should be considered if the pattern is atypical

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen trauma, weight-bearing ability, neurovascular status, and abdominal red flags
- Use walking aid or activity restriction advice if safe ambulation is impaired
- Escalate early if fracture, infection, or acute severe functional loss is suspected

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Perform focused hip, lumbar, and functional examination where appropriate
- Distinguish likely intra-articular, lateral hip, adductor, and referred pain patterns
- Begin load modification, gait advice, and graded strengthening or mobility work once serious pathology is excluded
- Refer for imaging or specialist review when trauma, major limp, or persistent function loss remains

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Hip pain in remote settings should be documented with clear weight-bearing status and transport advice
